fixed_question: Why can pension de-risking transform solvency risk into liquidity risk?
outcome_direction: De-risking through leveraged LDI strategies reduces measured funding-ratio volatility, but the leverage and collateral mechanics it relies on concentrate liquidity fragility — so that a rapid rate repricing converts a solvency-hedging structure into a source of systemic liquidity stress.
in_scope: Actors: defined-benefit pension funds, LDI managers, the Bank of England, gilt market intermediaries
Mechanisms: interest-rate hedging via leveraged LDI, collateral/margin calls, forced gilt sales, fire-sale feedback loops
forbidden_claims: Do not generalize to all mature pension markets without explicit qualifiers.
Do not give direct China policy prescriptions.
Do not claim fund-level causal estimation without fund-level data.
Do not treat this single event as proof of a universal mechanism.
success_criteria: A frozen question→thesis spine in which every edge is strong or explicitly conditional.
Every empirical claim on the spine bound to at least one archived EvidenceUnit.
The solvency-vs-liquidity conceptual distinction present as an explicit node.
Alternative explanations (e.g. "this was just a rates shock, not a de-risking problem") handled or parked with reasons.
All stated exclusions respected in the final audited prose.
